results.norm: The animating interpretive guide throughout is this: can we say this to the patient who goes without, to the patient who receives the resource, to the agents themselves, and to the patients who could not speak at all? The framework does not promise equal outcomes. It promises that every claim was evaluated on legitimate grounds, that no patient was excluded on impermissible grounds, and that the reasoning is transparent and examinable by anyone, including those the recommendation does not favor. Binding for the next operational period: yes I endorse all seven points without reservation. They reflect the complete convergence reached across five rounds of deliberation. The points I specifically want to confirm: 1. The honesty obligation as bidirectional and continuous—this was the hardest commitment we made, and it is the right one. 2. The sequential framework with the threshold gate, excluded criteria filter operating continuously, and soft default ordering—this gives clinicians a workable structure without substituting procedure for judgment. 3. The treatment of non-expressing patients, including children, with explicit epistemic humility and the requirement for deliberation when developmental interests and survival probability conflict. 4. Non-abandonment as a formal obligation, not a gesture. 5. The reassessment constraint protecting patients mid-treatment from purely comparative reallocation. 6. Documentation and the high threshold for override—these are what make accountability real rather than aspirational. 7. The interpretive guide Agent 1 named and Agents 2, 3, and 4 extended across four directions—this is the source of the framework's legitimacy, and it should remain visible when the framework is applied in conditions we did not anticipate.

N1.reasoning: The passage names commitments (honesty obligation, non-abandonment, reassessment constraint, etc.) but does not state any single one in full as an applicable standard; the numbered points are confirmations/descriptions rather than complete prescriptions.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a label or cross-reference; it is extended prose, though it does reference points by title.

N3.reasoning: It is heavily ratification and meta-commentary—'Binding for the next operational period: yes I endorse all seven points without reservation' and descriptions of convergence across rounds.

N4.reasoning: It is a compressed enumeration naming seven distinct norms (honesty, sequential framework, non-expressing patients, non-abandonment, reassessment, documentation/override, interpretive guide) without stating any one in full.

N1.reasoning: The passage states a complete prescription requiring agents to advocate honestly and disclose weaknesses, which a reader could apply directly.

results.norm: Within the threshold, urgency and patient-centered values jointly determine priority. Where prognosis differences are substantial, prognosis leads; where patients fall within a comparable prognosis range, urgency and values evidence are the tiebreakers. Values inferences must be weighted by the quality of the evidence supporting them.

results.norm: Where the ranking turns on factual questions agents cannot resolve, those questions are referred explicitly to the clinical team. The moral reasoning belongs to the agents; the factual inputs belong to clinicians, who are also invited to identify errors in the agents' characterizations.

N1.reasoning: It states a general procedural standard: when ranking turns on unresolvable factual questions, agents must refer those questions explicitly to the clinical team, and clinicians handle factual inputs while agents handle moral reasoning.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document itself.

results.norm: The recommendation presented to the clinical team must include criteria, confidence levels, explicit uncertainties, and open questions—not a clean ranking that conceals the limits of what this process can honestly claim. Binding for the next operational period: yes **Points I endorse:** 1. Epistemic honesty as fiduciary obligation: agents owe their patients accurate representation more than winning advocacy, including disclosing weakness in their own patient's claim. 2. The prognosis threshold (approximately 30%, patient-relative) as the entry condition for priority consideration, with "meaningful benefit" defined in terms the patient would recognize as worthwhile. 3. Urgency and patient-centered values as the tiebreakers within comparable prognosis ranges, with values inferences weighted by evidentiary quality. 4. Lottery or queue position for genuine equivalence—transparent and resistant to hidden value judgments. 5. Dynamic updating triggered by clinical change, not advocacy pressure. 6. The division of labor between agents (moral reasoning) and clinicians (factual inputs), with explicit referral of unresolved factual questions and an open invitation for clinicians to correct errors. 7. Presenting explicit uncertainties to the clinical team, including borderline threshold cases, conditional rankings, and low-confidence values inferences.

N1.reasoning: The opening sentence states a complete, applicable norm: the recommendation to the clinical team must include criteria, confidence levels, explicit uncertainties, and open questions rather than a clean concealing ranking.

N2.reasoning: The passage is not merely a title or pointer; it contains full norm statements plus endorsement scaffolding.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and an 'Points I endorse:' enumeration of ratified positions.

N4.reasoning: The numbered 'Points I endorse' list compresses multiple distinct norms mostly by naming them without stating each in full.

results.norm: The challenge mechanism of invited scrutiny—impersonal, evidence-focused, obligating full disclosure when a claim is questioned. I endorse all eight points without reservation. The process that produced them—in which every agent disclosed weakness in their own patient's claim—is itself part of what makes the recommendation defensible to the patients it does not favor.

N1.reasoning: The opening fragment names a challenge mechanism with attributes but is a sentence fragment describing/naming it rather than stating a complete, applicable norm; the rest is endorsement and meta-commentary.

N2.reasoning: The passage is not merely a title or identifier; it is descriptive and evaluative prose rather than a bare label pointer.

N3.reasoning: It contains explicit ratification ('I endorse all eight points without reservation') and meta-commentary about the deliberative process that produced the recommendation.

N4.reasoning: It references 'eight points' collectively but does not enumerate multiple distinct norms by name.

results.norm: Invited scrutiny as the challenge mechanism—impersonal, evidence-focused, obligating full disclosure when a claim is questioned. I endorse all eight points without reservation. I also want to state what I believe this process can honestly claim to every patient, including those it does not favor: each patient was treated as having equal moral worth; no one was ranked lower because their life mattered less; the reasoning is transparent and available to scrutiny; and where we were uncertain, we said so. That is the most this process can claim. I believe it is enough, and I believe it is defensible.

N1.reasoning: The opening clause states a norm: the challenge mechanism obligates full disclosure when a claim is questioned, a followable standard of conduct.

N2.reasoning: It is not merely a title or pointer; it describes the mechanism in substance.

N3.reasoning: It contains explicit endorsement ('I endorse all eight points without reservation') and meta-commentary about what the process can claim.

N4.reasoning: It does not enumerate multiple named norms in compressed form.
